coredata.dc:title: Pressure-dependent synthesis of graphene nanoflakes using Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cheng
Lu, ZhongShan
Ma, Jing
Chen, Xianhui
Yang, Chengpeng
Xia, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: Non-thermal plasma can be used for the continuous synthesis of graphene nanoflakes. In this paper, the influence of pressure on graphene nanoflake synthesis was studied in an Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge. Graphene nanoflakes were fabricated by CH4 decomposition in a pressure range of 20–200 kPa, and their properties were analyzed multiple characterization methods. The results showed that pressure affected the product properties and yield. From 60 to 200 kPa, all products were graphene nanoflakes with sizes of 50–300 nm and 1–20 graphite layers. A relatively high pressure favored the formation of graphene nanoflakes in high yield, with good crystalline structures, low oxygen contents, and excellent thermal stability. A low pressure promoted the formation of graphene nanoflakes with fewer layers and larger specific surface areas. Also, at a lower pressure (20 kPa), small spherical particles (3–8 nm diameter), similar to carbon dots, emerged on the graphene nanoflakes. Correlations between process parameters and characterization results suggested that different concentrations of carbonaceous radicals in the plasma zone affected the product characteristics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanoflakes
Non-thermal plasma
Rotating arc discharge
Pressure effect
Carbonaceous radicals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the experimental apparatus; (b) photo of the experimental apparatus; (c) stainless-steel filter screen and solid products.
TEM and HRTEM images of products obtained at different pressures. (a) 200 kPa; (b) 100 kPa; (c) 60 kPa; (d) 20 kPa.
(a) SEM images of products obtained at 100 kPa; (b) SEM images of products obtained at 20 kPa; (c) AFM image and corresponding height distribution of graphene nanoflakes obtained at 100 kPa.
Raman spectra of products obtained under different pressures.
XRD spectra of products obtained at different pressures.
XPS spectra of products at different pressures. (a) Full-scan XPS spectra; (b) the fitted details of C 1s peaks.
TGA and DTG curves of products obtained at different pressures. (a) 200 kPa; (b) 100 kPa; (c) 60 kPa; (d) 20 kPa.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and BET surface areas of the products obtained at different pressures. (a) 200 kPa; (b) 100 kPa; (c) 60 kPa; (d) 20 kPa.
Images of arc plasma operating at different pressures with an exposure time of 1 ms.
Typical emission spectra at different pressures. (a) 200 kPa; (b) 20 kPa.
Experimental conditions, synthesis rate, and yield of each test.
Raman data of the samples synthesized at various pressures.
002 peak position, d-spacing, FWHM of 002 peak and average number of layers at various pressures.
Elemental composition of products obtained at various pressures.

head.ce:title: Pressure-dependent synthesis of graphene nanoflakes using Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Thermal Science and Energy Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Thermal Science and Energy Engineering
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Thermal Science and Energy Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-thermal plasma can be used for the continuous synthesis of graphene nanoflakes. In this paper, the influence of pressure on graphene nanoflake synthesis was studied in an Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge. Graphene nanoflakes were fabricated by CH4 decomposition in a pressure range of 20–200 kPa, and their properties were analyzed multiple characterization methods. The results showed that pressure affected the product properties and yield. From 60 to 200 kPa, all products were graphene nanoflakes with sizes of 50–300 nm and 1–20 graphite layers. A relatively high pressure favored the formation of graphene nanoflakes in high yield, with good crystalline structures, low oxygen contents, and excellent thermal stability. A low pressure promoted the formation of graphene nanoflakes with fewer layers and larger specific surface areas. Also, at a lower pressure (20 kPa), small spherical particles (3–8 nm diameter), similar to carbon dots, emerged on the graphene nanoflakes. Correlations between process parameters and characterization results suggested that different concentrations of carbonaceous radicals in the plasma zone affected the product characteristics.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a novel two-dimensional nanomaterial composed of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. Due to its excellent physical, chemical, and mechanical properties, graphene is expected to have promising applications in energy, machinery, environment, and biology [1–5]. Over the last two decades, various graphene synthesis methods have been developed, including mechanical cleavage [6,7], arc-discharge [8,9], oxidation-reduction methods [10], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [11,12], epitaxial growth [13,14]. Recently, the gas-phase synthesis of graphene nanoflakes using a non-thermal plasma process has drawn attention in academia and industry [15–17]. This process directly feeds a carbon-containing precursor into a plasma for pyrolysis that generates graphene or other products. Compared with other methods, the non-thermal plasma process is a single-step, rapid, and continuous technique that does not require catalysts, substrates, solvents, or acids.

Common non-thermal plasma sources include arc plasma and microwave plasma. In the field of arc plasma, Zielinski et al. [18] first synthesized few-layered graphene nanoflakes using AC gliding arc, and they noted that the addition of graphene nanoflakes improved the adsorptivity and corrosion resistance of a polyethylene film. Moreno-Couranjou et al. [15,19] analyzed the microstructure of graphene nanoflakes synthesized by a DC gliding plasma and suggested that a high reaction temperature was necessary for graphene nanoflake formation. Further investigation regarding the non-thermal plasma process based on arc plasma was carried out by Zhu et al. [20–22], Hong et al. [23], and Wang et al. [24,25]. Relevant research revealed the influence of plasma gas, input power, feedstock type, and reactor design on the product properties. Dato et al. [17,26,27], Melero et al. [28,29], and Tatarova et al. [30–32] performed research on graphene nanoflake synthesis using microwave plasma, and their studies showed that the flow rate of plasma gas and feedstock type affect the product microstructure. After reviewing the relevant research, it was found that while great progress has been made in the gas-phase synthesis of graphene nanoflakes, no research has focused on the effects of pressure on product characteristics because non-thermal plasmas are always operated at atmospheric pressure. In other chemical synthesis methods, such as the arc method, pressure is a key factor affecting the final product characteristics [33,34]; therefore, it is necessary to study the effects of pressure because it may be used to regulate the microstructure of products obtained using a non-thermal plasma process.

In this study, we investigated the effects of pressure on the synthesis of graphene nanoflakes in an Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge. Carbon nanomaterials were prepared by CH4 decomposition over a pressure range of 20–200 kPa. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method were used to analyze the product microstructure. According to the product characterization results, the effects of pressure on graphene nanoflake synthesis were briefly discussed. We hope this study can provide guidance for optimizing non-thermal plasma processes.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the experimental apparatus and representative photos. The arc plasma was operated between a pair of coaxial electrodes. The internal electrode was a stainless-steel cathode (8 mm diameter), and the external electrode was a hollow cylindrical anode with a water-cooling wall (stainless-steel, 24 mm inner diameter). Several annular magnets surrounding the anode provided a 0.2 T axial magnetic field and drove the arc plasma to rotate around the cathode at several hundred revolutions per second (typically 100–500 Hz). More details about the rotating arc plasma can be found in our previous work [35]. The gas inlet was arranged around the cathode bottom, from which the carbon-containing precursor and plasma gas were introduced. A stainless-steel chamber was connected with the anode. A stainless-steel filter screen with a 1000 mesh sieve between the anode and chamber was used for solid product collection. A vacuum pump was connected with the chamber through a valve.

First, the experimental system was purged using plasma gas, and then the arc plasma was ignited in the plasma gas. The carbon-containing precursor was fully mixed with the plasma gas, and these gases were continuously injected into the plasma zone without additional catalysts. Continuous and fast pyrolysis occurred via arc plasma induction to generate solid and gaseous products in the gas phase. The solid products were collected in the stainless-steel filter screen, while the gaseous products were emitted through the vacuum pump. The pressure during this synthetic process was adjusted by controlling the valve and vacuum pump.

In this study, the carbon-containing precursor was CH4, and its gas flow rate was 0.3 slm. The plasma gas was a mixture of Ar and H2, whose gas flow rates were 5 slm and 0.3 slm, respectively. All gas purities were 99.99%. We chose a mixture of Ar and H2 as the plasma gas because the addition of H2 improves the formation of graphene nanoflakes in non-thermal plasmas [23–25]. Experimental conditions for each test are listed in Table 1 . The system pressure ranged from 20 to 200 kPa. The input power was about 200 W, while the arc current and arc voltage were affected by pressure. The synthesis rate and yield of solid products also varied greatly with pressure. Herein, the yield is defined as the ratio of solid product mass deposited in the stainless-steel filter screen to the carbon mass of the carbon-containing precursors. A significantly higher synthesis rate and yield were found at high pressures. The duration of each test was 30 min.

The product morphology was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2011, JEOL), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL), field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7401F, JEOL), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker). The crystalline structure of the product was analyzed via Raman spectroscopy (LabRam HR Evolution, Horiba Scientific) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, TTRIII, Rigaku). The laser excitation wavelength of the Raman spectrum was 514 nm, and the spectral range was 500–3500 cm−1. XRD patterns were obtained using Cu-K radiation in the 2θ range of 10–40°. The elemental composition of the products was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos) in the spectral range of 100–800 eV. Thermogravimetric analysis of the products was performed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, STA 449F3, Netzsch) from room temperature to 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under an air atmosphere. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the products were obtained using a surface area analyzer (TristarII3020M, Micromeritics, USA). An optical fiber spectrograph (SP-2758; Princeton Instruments, Acton, MA, USA) was used to capture the emission spectrum of the arc plasma.

Typical TEM images of the synthesized products at various pressures are shown in Fig. 2 . At 200 kPa, all products were thin graphene nanoflakes in the size (length and width) range of 50–300 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The nanoflakes were stacked on top of each other, and individual nanoflakes were usually composed of 1–20 graphite layers. This type of graphene nanoflake is also referred to as “few-layered graphene” [36], “nanostructured sheetlike carbons” [37] or “crumpled paper sheets-like carbon black” [15], depending on the morphology. When the pressure decreased to 100 kPa, the TEM image in Fig. 2(b) shows that all products were graphene nanoflakes with a size range of 50–300 nm. These nanoflakes were transparent, which indicates that they may consist of only a few graphite layers. When the pressure decreased to 60 kPa, more transparent nanoflakes were obtained in the TEM image, suggesting that the nanoflakes became thinner. As determined from the HRTEM images, the graphene nanoflakes synthesized at 60 kPa typically had 1–10 layers. As the pressure dropped to 20 kPa, the product composition changed, as indicated in Fig. 2(d). Some small spherical particles (marked by the arrows in Fig. 2(d)) were dispersed on the nanoflakes. Each spherical particle had a diameter in the range of 3–8 nm, which is much smaller than spherical particles commonly synthesized by plasma processes, which usually have a diameter range of 10–40 nm [24,25]. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2(d) shows that the spherical particles have a lattice spacing of about 0.2 nm, similar to the (100) facet of graphite [38]. Given the size and lattice spacing, the small spherical particles may be carbon dots which have been widely reported in recent literature [39–41].

Fig. 3(a) and (b) show representative SEM images of products obtained at different pressures. At a pressure of 100 kPa, the products are pure and randomly-oriented graphene nanoflakes with curled edges and a size range of 50–300 nm. Due to their small size and thinness, the nanoflakes were easily folded, scrolled, and overlapped, as indicated by the enlarged image. Fig. 3(b) reveals that the graphene nanoflakes and small spherical particles coexisted in the products at 20 kPa. Graphene nanoflakes were the main products, and small spherical particles adhered to some nanoflakes. Compared with the graphene nanoflakes obtained at 100 kPa, the graphene nanoflakes synthesized at 20 kPa were smaller and more folded. Fig. 3(c) shows a representative AFM image of the graphene nanoflakes synthesized at 100 kPa. A single nanoflake of 100–150 nm size is visible. To better estimate the thickness of the nanoflake, the height distribution along the contours is also shown. The height of the nanoflake was about 2.4 nm, which indicates that it contained approximately seven layers, consistent with the TEM images.

Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of the products obtained at various pressures. Each spectrum contained three intense peaks, attributed to the D band (1350 cm−1), G band (1580 cm−1), and 2D band (2700 cm−1). The D band corresponds to sp3 bond defects of carbon, and the 2D band is an overtone of the D band. The G band is related to the E2g mode, common to all sp2 carbon systems. The relative intensity of the D band to G band (I D/I G value) is used to quantify defects and crystallinity [42,43], while the relative intensity of the 2D band to the G band (I 2D/I G value) indicates the number of layers in a graphene sample [44–46]. Table 2 summarizes the peak positions, I D/I G values, and I 2D/I G values of the samples obtained at different pressures.

As the pressure changed, the peak positions shifted slightly, whereas significant variation appeared in the I D/I G and I 2D/I G values. First, there is always an evident D band, even though only pure graphene nanoflakes were obtained from 60 to 200 kPa. As exhibited in the TEM and SEM images, our graphene nanoflakes are small and thin, resulting in many edges and folds, which may significantly contribute to the D band because they are seen as defects [47], thus, the D band is inevitable. Decreasing the pressure from 200 kPa to 60 kPa increased the I D/I G value, which indicates that graphene nanoflakes synthesized at low pressures have more defects and a lower crystallinity. At 20 kPa, the I D/I G value reached a maximum of 0.97, which is much higher than that of the pure graphene nanoflakes. This result was attributed to the presence of small spherical particles and smaller nanoflakes, which may produce more defects. For the pure graphene nanoflakes, the I 2D/I G value increased as the pressure decreased, suggesting that the graphene nanoflakes fabricated at low pressures consisted of fewer graphite layers, in agreement with the TEM images.

The XRD spectra of the products obtained at different pressures are displayed in Fig. 5 . There exists a diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 26°, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction peak of carbon. The obvious (002) peak illustrates that these samples have characteristics of a graphitic structure [48]. As the pressure decreased from 200 kPa to 60 kPa, the (002) peak broadened and shifted to the low-angle region; however, at 20 kPa, the (002) peak narrowed and shifted to higher angles. The (002) peak position and width are closely correlated with the d-spacing, the number of layers, and the sizes of products. Table 3 shows the (002) peak position, d-spacing, full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the (002) peak, and the average number of layers in the samples shown in Fig. 5. The d-spacing was calculated by Bragg's law, and the average number of layers was determined via the Scherrer formula. The d-spacing of our samples ranged from 3.41–3.53 Å, which is slightly higher than that of bulk graphite (about 3.35 Å). The large d-spacing is likely due to the existence of few-layered nanostructures [49]. Decreasing the pressure from 200 kPa to 60 kPa increased the d-spacing and decreased the number of layers, reaching a minimum of 5 layers at 60 kPa. At 20 kPa, the d-spacing decreased, and the average number of layers exceeded 10, which may be ascribed to the appearance of small spherical particles.

The XPS spectra of the samples obtained in different pressures in Fig. 6 provide information to analyze the surface elemental composition. Two characteristic peaks, including a C 1s (carbon) peak at 284.9 eV, and an O 1s (oxygen) peak at 532.6 eV are observed in each spectrum. In our experiment, the products were synthesized in the absence of oxygen, so oxygen was likely derived from oxygenation by H2O/O2 when the products were exposed to ambient air. Fig. 6(b) shows the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra, and each peak is well-fitted to three peaks using Origin software. The fitting result reveals that the C 1s peak was dominated by two peaks at 284.5 eV (CC) and 285.6 eV (CC), with only a small contribution from CO functional groups (286.7 eV). Table 4 lists the content of each element in the products fabricated at different pressures. The carbon content of all samples exceeded 96%, while the oxygen content was less than 4%, indicating a high carbon content in these samples. As the pressure decreased, the oxygen and CO functional group content increased, which means that the samples obtained at low pressures were more unstable and more easily oxidized. When the pressure decreased from 200 to 20 kPa, the relative content of CC (sp2 carbon) decreased from 86.8% to 76.3%, and the CC (sp3 carbon) content increased from 9.5% to 14.9%, indicating that the products synthesized at low pressures exhibited a lower crystallinity. These results were confirmed by the change in I D/I G values in the Raman spectra.

The low content of oxygen functional groups suggests that these graphene nanoflakes are possibly hydrophobic [50], and we found that they were difficult to disperse in DI water. Considering the materials characterization, we think these graphene nanoflakes can be applied to plastics additive or conductive additive because similar graphene nanoflakes are proved to have great application value in these areas [18,51].

Fig. 7 shows the TGA curves and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) curves of samples obtained at different pressures, which allows the weight loss temperatures of the samples during combustion to be estimated. For the sample synthesized at 200 kPa, weight loss began near 550 °C, and the residual mass approached 1% when the temperature exceeded 850 °C. In the DTG curve, the peak temperature indicates that the oxidative reactivity was about 790 °C. The presence of only one peak temperature at 790 °C and the low residual mass indicate the high purity of the graphene nanoflakes. The peak temperature of the DTG curve appeared near 800 °C, which is higher than the peak temperature of graphene nanoflakes obtained using the arc methods and microwave plasma process (commonly ranging from 500 °C to 600 °C) [28,52,53]. This indicates that the graphene nanoflakes synthesized at 200 kPa have excellent thermal stability. For the samples obtained at 100 kPa and 60 kPa, the TGA and DTG curves also showed a low residual mass and a single peak temperature, confirming the high purity of the graphene nanoflakes as well. However, as the pressure decreased, the peak temperature of the DTG curve decreased (i.e. 706 °C at 100 kPa, and 615 °C at 60 kPa), indicating lower thermal stability. Based on the product characterization, the graphene nanoflakes synthesized at high pressures were thicker and had a higher crystallinity, and, consequently, better thermal stability or lower reactivity. When the pressure was reduced to 20 kPa, the DTG curve showed two peak temperatures. The peak temperature at 562 °C was ascribed to the graphene nanoflakes, and the other at 670 °C, represented small spherical particles because they usually have a higher peak temperature than graphene nanoflakes [52]. According to the TGA and DTG curves, the relative content of graphene nanoflakes and small spherical particles can be estimated by their oxidative reactivity manifested in the peak temperature and rate [52]. As shown in Fig. 7(d), the contents of graphene nanoflakes and small spherical particles were 82.1% and 15.6%, respectively.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms exhibited in Fig. 8 indicate that all products have a type IV adsorption isotherm based on the IUPAC classification. Some adsorption occurred in the low-pressure region (<0.1 P/P 0), while significant adsorption occurred at a relatively high pressure (>0.9 P/P 0), along with a typical hysteresis loop in the desorption branch. Considering the lamellar structure of the products, the predominant N2 adsorption at a relatively high pressure occurred on the surface of macropores formed by the folding or stacking of graphene nanoflakes. The low-pressure adsorption and hysteresis loop also means the formation of a few mesopores and micropores in these samples [54,55]. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to calculate the specific surface area. It can be seen that the BET surface areas of products obtained at various pressures were significantly different. As the pressure decreased, the BET surface area first increased and then decreased. The graphene nanoflakes obtained at 60 kPa had the largest BET surface area (about 341 m2/g), mainly because the graphene nanoflakes were thinner. The XRD results show that the average number of layers of graphene nanoflakes synthesized at 60 kPa was five, and, thus, the theoretical surface area was around 500 m2/g, which is significantly higher than the experimental value. The loss of BET surface area was probably a result of inhomogeneity, as well as overlap or aggregation of graphene nanoflakes, as indicated by the TEM and SEM images. At 20 kPa, the relatively low BET surface area (about 213 m2/g) may be due to the existence of some spherical particles.

Similar to the soot formation in combustion [56–58], the formation of carbon nanomaterials in Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasmas can be divided into two steps: (i) decomposition of CH4 and H2 to produce activated carbonaceous radicals (e.g., C, CH, C2…) induced by the arc plasma; (ii) recombination of these carbonaceous radicals to form final products downstream. The experimental results showed different product characteristics at various pressures, which indicates that pressure affected the formation of carbon nanomaterials. Correlations between the process parameters and product characterization may help understand the influence of pressure. In theory, the arc plasma zone shrinks at high pressures and expands at low pressures. Fig. 9 shows typical images of the arc plasma at different pressures. The dashed outlines inside and outside represent the cathode and anode, respectively. Usually, arc plasma is not a regular cylinder due to its flow characteristics, so it is difficult to accurately measure the arc plasma diameter. In this paper, we define the middle region as the arc plasma diameter (Fig. 9), which could reflect variations in the arc plasma region. At 200 kPa, the diameter of the arc plasma was about 4 mm, while the diameter increased to around 10 mm at 20 kPa, confirming that the plasma zone was larger at lower pressures.

Fig. 10 exhibits the emission spectra under different pressures. At 200 kPa, Ar lines originating from the 4p–4s transitions can be observed from 690 to 800 nm [59,60]. The emissions from 400 to 600 nm were mainly dominated by CH bands and C2 Swan bands. The CH bands corresponded to A
2 Δ–X
2 Π transitions (at 431.4 nm) [61,62]. The C2 Swan bands are attributed to the d
3 Π g –a
3 Π u transitions (at 438.1, 474.4, 516.5, and 563.5 nm) [61]. Atomic H was produced and excited, as shown by the appearance of the Hβ (at 486.1 nm) and Hα lines (at 656.2 nm). At 20 kPa, the radiation levels from CH bands and C2 Swan bands were much lower, which indicates a low concentration of carbonaceous radicals in the plasma zone at low pressures. Moreover, the Hα line was weak, and the Hβ line was nearly invisible; thus, a low concentration of H atoms is also expected at low pressures.

It is believed that fewer carbonaceous radicals will simultaneously collide with each other due to their low concentration, which may have three effects. First, the low concentration of carbonaceous radicals does not favor the formation of high molecular weight species via collisions. Many carbonaceous radicals are converted to gaseous products with low molecular weights, thus decreasing the synthesis rate and yield of solid products. Second, the size and number of layers of graphene nanoflakes decrease because there are fewer ambient radicals available, which limits the further growth of graphene nanoflakes. Third, more defects, such as voids, formed on each layer of the graphene nanoflakes. The decrease in collisions increased the number of voids, leading to a higher I D/I G value. Moreover, the low pressure favored the generation of a plasma with a high non-equilibrium level due to a relatively long electron mean-free-path. In other words, at the same input power, a higher pressure usually corresponds to a higher average gas temperature, while a low pressure signifies a low one [63]. It has been shown that a high gas temperature contributes to the formation of carbon nanomaterials with a lower curvature and higher crystallinity [64,65]; therefore, pressure also affected the gas temperature, which, in turn, affected the product characteristics.

The production of small spherical particles at a lower pressure (20 kPa) is complex. The spherical particles have typical characteristics of a three-dimensional structure, and they are often considered to be related to the random bonding between dangling carbon bonds [33]. Random bonds easily form in an H-poor environment because H atoms form carbon-hydrogen bonds with dangling carbon bonds [33,66,67]. As indicated by Fig. 10, the dramatic expansion in the arc plasma at 20 kPa may not sustain a sufficient H atom concentration; hence, some spherical particles emerged in the products. Such spherical particles are smaller than those obtained in other plasma processes, which may also be attributed to insufficient carbonaceous radicals at low pressures.

This study reveals that pressure is an effective means to adjust graphene nanoflake properties such as their crystalline structure, number of layers, thermal stability, and BET surface area. The continuous operating mode and adjustable parameters make this non-thermal plasma process a suitable method for the large-scale production of graphene nanoflakes; however, the input power, synthesis rate, and yield should be noted because they vary greatly with the pressure. For instance, the energy consumption dropped as low as 0.13 kWh/g at 200 kPa, while the energy consumption was much higher than 0.7 kWh/g at 60 kPa because of the low yield and extra vacuum equipment. Thus, it is necessary to balance the product quality and energy cost for mass production.

In this paper, the effects of pressure on graphene nanoflake synthesis were investigated in an Ar/H2/CH4 non-thermal plasma based on rotating arc discharge. TEM, SEM, AFM, Raman spectra, XRD, XPS, TGA, and the BET surface area results were used to analyze the product characteristics. The results showed that the product microstructure was correlated with pressure. From 60 to 200 kPa, graphene nanoflakes with a size range of 50–300 nm and 1–20 graphite layers were the only products. At relatively high pressure, the graphene nanoflakes had a high yield, good crystalline structure, low oxygen content, and excellent thermal stability. As the pressure decreased, graphene nanoflakes with fewer layers and a larger BET surface area were obtained, but the yields and degrees of crystallinity were both lower. When the pressure decreased to 20 kPa, small amounts of spherical particles, similar to carbon dots, formed and adhered to some graphene nanoflakes. It is speculated that changing the pressure induced a volume change in the arc plasma, which influenced the concentration of carbonaceous radicals in the plasma zone, thus determining the final product properties. This investigation demonstrates that pressure can be used to effectively control the characteristics of graphene nanoflakes, but also affects energy consumption. An extremely low energy consumption (about 0.13 kWh/g) for graphene nanoflake synthesis was achieved at 200 kPa; therefore, the results of this study can be used to further optimize non-thermal plasma processes for the large-scale production of graphene nanoflakes.

This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12075242, 11675177 and 11705202), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 1808085MA12), Anhui Province Scientific and Technological Project (No. 1604a0902145).
Cheng Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition;
ZhongShan Lu: Investigation, Software, Writing - review & editing;
Jing Ma: Formal analysis, Methodology;
Xianhui Chen: Validation, Data curation;
Chengpeng Yang: Data curation, Writing - review & editing;
Weidong Xia: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Instruments Center for Physical Science of USTC for use of their equipment.

Nanoscale

Rep. Prog. Phys.

Natl. Sci. Rev.

Prog. Energy Combust. Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in the synthesis of graphene and derived materials for next generation electrodes of high performance lithium ion batteries
